questions.question: You want to add a new line to an existing log file without erasing its contents. Which mode should you pass to open()?

options.explanation: Append mode adds new content to the end of the file, leaving existing content alone.
Write mode truncates the file to zero bytes before writing — every existing line is gone.
Read mode doesn't allow writing at all. You'd get an io.UnsupportedOperation error on the first write.
"rw" is not a valid mode string in Python. The read-write modes are "r+" and "w+", but neither one is right here — "w+" would still erase the file first.

questions.question: You read a file line by line and check if line == "Alice". The condition is always False even though "Alice" is in the file. What's the most likely cause?

options.text: Each line from the file ends with "\n", so line is "Alice\n", not "Alice"
options.explanation: File iterators keep the trailing newline on each line. Call line.strip() — or line.rstrip() — before comparing.

options.text: Python's == operator doesn't work on file data
options.explanation: == works fine on strings from files. The issue is a content mismatch, not the operator.

options.text: The file must be opened in binary mode to compare strings
options.explanation: Binary mode would give you bytes objects, which would make comparisons harder, not easier.

options.text: You need to call f.read() first to load the file into memory
options.explanation: Iterating over a file object line by line is correct and efficient. The bug is the forgotten strip, not the iteration method.

questions.question: You have a CSV file where one row is: "Smith, John",88. What happens if you parse it with line.strip().split(",")?

options.text: You get three fields: '"Smith', ' John"', and '88' — the name is split across two entries
options.explanation: split(",") doesn't understand CSV quoting rules. Use csv.reader or csv.DictReader, which handle quoted fields correctly.

options.text: You get two fields correctly: 'Smith, John' and '88'
options.explanation: split(",") splits on every comma regardless of quoting. The name contains a comma, so it gets split too.

options.text: Python raises a ValueError because the field contains a comma
options.explanation: split() never raises ValueError — it just splits on every occurrence of the delimiter, with no awareness of quoting.

options.text: You get one field containing the entire line
options.explanation: split(",") would split on the commas, not ignore them. You'd get too many pieces, not one.
